summary: DHH argues that America's problems are real but overstated, pushing back against the prevailing narrative of decline. He uses data on gun deaths, median incomes, and Millennial wealth to show that many metrics are better than popular discourse suggests. Drawing on his experience living in Denmark, he contends that Americans romanticize Nordic societies without understanding the material trade-offs involved. He concludes that much of America's malaise is a self-inflicted vibe problem rather than a reflection of unprecedented decline, and calls for a realistic restoration of national self-confidence.
key_points: Gun violence and homicide rates, while high, are below historical peaks from the 1970s-90s, contradicting narratives of unprecedented crisis
Millennial median incomes are significantly above past generations in constant dollars, and homeownership rates are roughly comparable
Americans romanticize Nordic countries without reckoning with the broad-based taxes, smaller living spaces, and material sacrifices that fund those systems
Much of America's current malaise is a vibe-driven phenomenon rather than a reflection of actual unprecedented decline
A strong, self-confident America matters not just domestically but for global stability, including for the Nordic societies often held up as alternatives
money_quotes: America is a violent country. It's been a violent country for a long time. This is not a novel situation.
On purely median-income terms, all boats are rising in the US, even if the biggest boats are rising faster.
I think some Americans have a tendency to romanticize the Nordics. And to think all that's needed to turn the US into a similar society is just to tax some billionaires more. That's just nonsense.
Plenty of it also just stems from the general vibe that things are terrible because they feel terrible. And that's the part that seems self-inflicted, unnecessary, and correctable.
America needs to get its mojo back. Its self-confidence. Not the starry-eyed, see-no-evil-hear-no-evil kind, but the realistic kind.
I don't actually want to see where the world might go if a strong America is replaced on the world scene by the current prospective alternatives.
It took a break from being in the thick of it to realize that, yes, I am actually on Team America. Proudly so.
key_insight: America's malaise is largely a self-inflicted vibe problem that obscures genuinely strong long-term trends, and restoring realistic self-confidence matters both domestically and globally.
